Copying comment to more visible position in conversation:

I recall you asked about this earlier. I've looked through the OpenSSL code, and the logic around MTU determination sure has changed a lot. I think that just setting this to the 802.3 default won't work, though: there can be many reasons that path MTU might be lower than this. One common scenario is DSL: PPPoE induces an 8-byte overhead, reducing the MTU to 1492 right at the network border. I think that PyDTLS would just fail now with this hardcode in such situations.

In bss_dgram.c I see that on Linux, OpenSSL tries to determine the MTU from sockopt IP_MTU as part of BIO_CTRL_DGRAM_QUERY_MTU. On Windows, this path is not taken, and this query just returns 0. The caller then falls into a different code path, which computes a minimum by subtracting a protocol-dependent overhead from a guess.

So I think we need to trace though this and find out why that's not working on Windows. Would you like to do that with a debug version of the OpenSSL dll's you built?

> there needs to be a lua shared library

Wouldn't the header files be enough? It shouldn't normally have to link against the lua library, because that is provided (and already loaded) by Lupa.

I would consider a pull request that makes Lupa ship the header files of the Lua version that it was built with or against, and give it a function `lupa.get_includes()` that returns the (list of) directories needed for the build.

That being said, I'd also be ok with switching to a dynamic build and shipping liblua, if that's what it takes. Pull requests that work on all major platforms will be considered.

> One last comment on the wheels, and Cython, and inter-operability ... I saw a discussion on SageMath regarding the use of statically linked versus dynamically linked extensions. My only concern in static vs. dynamic is ensuring that Cython code (probably equivalent to saying all-of-Sage) is using the GMP, MPFR, and MPC libraries as gmpy2. At the moment, the C-API is disabled with a static build of gmpy2. I don't mind enabling the C-API for a static build. Please let me know if you want me to make the change.

Make sense to me.

> (MPFR/MPC could be tricky because the precision is a global variable. The current version of gmpy2 expects the precision to be set to the maximum for the calculation and then rounds the result down to the desired precision. During the rounding process, the existing precision is saved and then restored. If this is different than Sage's usage of the MPFR library, I'll need to change gmpy2 to always set the precision, at least on a dynamically linked build. I probably should do that anyway. Note that these concerns aren't applicable to GMP. I've already disabled changing the memory manager functions in GMP.)

In Sage, there is no global variable for precision. Each mpfr carries a given precision and you can only perform operations between mpfr elements with the same precision. If two elements are added and have different precisions, then the one with largest precision is truncated. There is no global variable.

I don't quite understand the usage of the global precision variable in gmpy2.

caffe's load_image seems to return floats in the range 0-1.0, while the library usually expects uint8 in the range 0-255. So you would have to do something like `(img*255).astype(np.uint8)` after loading the images.
Considering your image size of 32x32, the number of augmenters is also rather large. You should probably decrease each augmenters probability by changing `st = lambda aug: iaa.Sometimes(0.3, aug)` to `st = lambda aug: iaa.Sometimes(0.1, aug)`.
Further, you should use something like `seq.show_grid(images[0], rows=8, cols=8)` to show your augmenters results instead of `plt.imshow(images_aug[0])`, as the latter one will result in the same augmentation at every script run.

I think this case is not generic enough to warrant being included in Faker. In other words, I don't think many people would find that useful.

You could still package your provider as a stand-alone Python package, and use it with faker.

Assuming your provider has an import path of `course_provider.Provider`:

```
from course_provider import Provider as CourseProvider
from faker import Faker

fake = Faker()
fake.add_provider(CourseProvider)

fake.grade()
```

> Isn't the purpose of six backwards compatibility? :smiley:

Not exactly. The purpose is Python 2&3 compatibility. If something can simply be rewritten in a modern way compatible with Python 2&3, six isn't needed.  `six.moves` exists because there's no native way to deal with Python 3 module-renamings besides writing a lot of `try: ... except ImportError: ...`. Not all of six is aligned with this philosophy, but I'm increasingly keeping to it as Python 2 ages.

> The context of this is that I'm developing a comprehensive list of moves and removals between python 2 & 3. One of the attributes I'm trying to be careful about is whether it's a "drop-in replacement", and this move was the only thing keeping six.moves.urllib.parse from being a drop-in replacement for urlparse. In other words, adding this means you can say "replace all imports of urlparse with six.moves.urllib.parse and it will work."

Hmm, I suppose this is true for `urllib_parse` because Python 2's `urlparse` is a subset of Python 3's `urllib.parse`. In general, though, the `urllib(2)` moves are quite a mess, and I'm not sure if you're able to make any such nice definitive statements.

> Also, while I agree it's undocumented in Python, it'll also be undocumented in six, so it's not exactly "enshrining" it.

Ha, (six) documentation is definitely a prerequisite for merging this.

> In any case, please feel free to close this PR if you don't find these arguments convincing. Thanks again for writing this library!

Thanks for the kind words and understanding. Looking further, I don't think comment I was eying yesterday actually applies to `scheme_chars`. Certainly, it's unlikely to go anywhere anytime soon. So, I suppose we can move ahead on this.

It looks like the (my) code naively presumes that the base is in the range of 0..255, although other values can accidentally work they may read from outside the lookup table. Actually it's worse than that and even values in that range can result in reading garbage. Reading garbage will result in seemingly ok results on some platforms (it is a pseudo-random function after all), but on others with stricter memory access it crashes as you noted.

Anyway it's fixable but the internal api will need an adjustment. For some reason base is currently an integer, which only makes sense if you subscribe to the rather insane presumptions of the present code. It would be better for it to be a float and have it interact with the coordinates before they are mapped to the lookup table.

I'll work on a fix, but for now you can workaround by adding the base value to the input coordinates yourself, and leaving base at zero, which should not crash. The base value concept is a bit dubious anyway, and the implementation doubly so. Some self flagellation is possibly in order as well.

The HTTP socket exceptions were solved by running the provider under the production "wsgidav" binary, they only appeared when running in development mode (just as they do when using any wsgi framework), so it's not a bug.

In the end, I didn't touch at all the source code of WsgiDAV. I worked-around the seek() call by just returning a wrapper "file-like" object which created the proxied HTTP range request call.

But there is just one down-side to this, which would be awesome if it can be addressed: because we only receive the "range_start" position, the HTTP GET call done by the provider will actually request an indefinite amount of data, instead of a fixed length. While not an actual bug (everything works perfectly), this kind of breaks connection pooling when seeking through a file (because the previous connection has to be force-closed as it has not finished reading the entire data that the server keeps pushing down).

How much of a trouble would be to add a second argument to seek, considering how other providers work? The line in question would be [this one](https://github.com/mar10/wsgidav/blob/master/wsgidav/request_server.py#L1475).

Hi ksheurs, yes you can log exceptions by looking at the exception property is the state context. For example:

```ruby
require "durable"

Durable.ruleset :flow do
  when_all m.action == "start" do
    raise "Unhandled Exception!"
  end
  
  # when the exception property exists
  when_all +s.exception do
    puts "#{s.exception}"
    s.exception = nil
  end

  when_start do
    post :flow, { :action => "start" }
  end
end

Durable.run_all
```

So the problem with making it a warning is that warnings are basically useless in the Python testing ecosystem - nose or unit.test don't attach them to the tests and py.test will swalow them if the test passes.

The point of making them an error isn't to make them mandatory, but to make them unmissable when they happen. If your tests are failing the health checks and you think this is legit, you can disable the health checks on a per test basis with settings(perform_health_check=False). Yes, if they're module functions you'd have to import them along with the decorator and field().

from dataclass import dataclass, field, asdict

The usual reason for allowing fields with underscores is in the dynamic class creation case, where you're reading the fields from a database or CSV file. But it's not much of an argument, because you have to be able to remap field names, anyway. If a field is named "class" or other keyword, you'll have bigger problems. namedtuple has an "automatic field rename on collision or keyword" feature. I'm not sure how attrs handles it, if at all.

Ignoring the dynamic case, I'd be okay with saying fields can't start with an underscore, and then using _asdict, _fields, etc. Or module functions, I don't feel too strongly about it. Member functions makes for a better API, module functions gives more leeway to the user to pick names.

You can write your own method to use the session ID as the ratelimit key. `key` supports [callables and dotted paths to callables](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/keys.html#callable-values).

Ratelimiting by session ID provides too little utility to be in the core.

* If the user is logged in, you can use the user ID.
* If the user is not logged in, they can change their session ID at will by erasing or ignoring the cookie.

(Yes, it's possible to spoof IP addresses, but if someone is going to that trouble you have to assume they're willing to ignore session cookies, too.)

I use ratelimit on a site that gets a lot of traffic from schools, who are probably the biggest NAT offenders. This is exactly why ratelimit's [default is *not* to block](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#ratelimit) requests, but to annotate them so you could, e.g., require a captcha. I also mention NAT in the [Security Considerations](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html#network-address-translation) section of the docs.

(python-)RtMidi is very low-level. The `MidiOut.send_message`` method expects the MIDI bytes exactly as they go over the wire, which means the channel is encoded in the lower four bits of channel mode message status bytes. Since four bits can represent values from 0-15, the channel is zero-based, but the "logical" channel counting in the MIDI spec is one-based (i.e. from 1 - 16).

It's easy to write a wrapper for `MidiOut` that offers convenience methods for sending common midi messages and handles passing the MIDI channel more intuitively.

Here's a quick example:

```#!python
import time
import rtmidi
from rtmidi.midiconstants import *


class MidiOut(rtmidi.MidiOut):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.channel = kwargs.pop('ch', 1)
        super(MidiOut, self).__init__( *args, **kwargs)

    def channel_message(self, command, *data, ch=None):
        """Send a MIDI channel mode message."""
        command = (command & 0xf0) | ((ch if ch else self.channel) - 1 & 0xf)
        msg = [command] + [value & 0x7f for value in data]
        self.send_message(msg)

    def note_off(self, note, velocity=0, ch=None):
        """Send a 'Note Off' message."""
        self.channel_message(NOTE_OFF, note, velocity, ch=ch)

    def note_on(self, note, velocity=0, ch=None):
        """Send a 'Note Off' message."""
        self.channel_message(NOTE_OFF, note, velocity, ch=ch)

    def program_change(self, program, bank=None, msb=None, lsb=None, ch=None):
        """Send a 'Program Change' message."""
        self.channel_message(PROGRAM_CHANGE, program, ch=ch)


mout = MidiOut(ch=3)
mout.open_virtual_port()
input('Press key...')
mout.program_change(40)
mout.note_on(60)
time.sleep(1)
mout.note_off(60)
```

I think the problem might be how the library is passing the PORT in the connection string to `pyodbc`. The PORT is passed using `PORT=`:

    DRIVER=ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server;PORT=32771;SERVER=localhost;UID=sa;PWD=P@ssW0rd!;DATABASE=test-db;MARS_Connection=yes

Where generally the PORT should be included after the server with a comma:

    DRIVER=ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server;SERVER=localhost,32771;UID=sa;PWD=P@ssW0rd!;DATABASE=test-db;MARS_Connection=yes

I don't see any documentation at https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki supporting `PORT=` as a valid way to pass the port number.

I haven't looked through the `pyodbc` source yet the see if it's an undocumented feature. But fixing the port number in the connection string produced by `django-pyodbc-azure` is a valid connection string for `pyodbc`

After thinking about this more, and coming up with more and more edge cases that would have to be gracefully dealt with, I am going to stand by my original decision to not make these claims optional in this extension. It would complicate the code too much for my liking, and make it harder to do additional changes to this codebase in the future.

Instead, let me introduce `Flask-JWT-Simple` (https://github.com/vimalloc/flask-jwt-simple). It is a toned down version of this extension with all the opinionated stuff needed for the extra features ripped out. This new extension allow you full control over claims when creating JWTs, and places no restrictions on what claims need to be in consumed JWTs.

If there are features missing from the simple variant of this extension that you would like to see, I would be more then happy to port them over. The two that I could see being helpful right off hand is JWT blacklisting and JWT claim verification. Both of those should work fine in this non-opinionated extension.

Hopefully this will be sufficient for your use case. If you have any problems or concerns, don't hesitate to let me know.

Cheers.

If I was approaching this, there are a couple of things I would take into consideration.

- Most custom formatters (currently) don't need the highlight options, so I would make it...well optional.  I imagine extended `custom_fences` dictionary to have a key named something like `pass_hl_options`.  Probably access it with `.get` so you can assign a default value of `False` if the key isn't present.  This will help preserve backwards compatibility.

- Then you would conditionally pass the options into the format only if we originally got `True` for `pass_hl_options` on **that** custom fence.  I don't want to force people to change existing formatters to accept additional option(s) they don't need, so it would be better to pass them only when required.

- When passing the options, I'd pass them as single parameter that is a dictionary.  Each option would be accessed via their normal key name.  I'd go ahead and pass all the available options just for the sake of consistency.

So in short, I'm not against such a feature. SuperFences essentially evolved from my own needs at the time, but if we can extend it in a generalized way that can assist additional, advanced use cases, I'm fine with that.  Since it could help with specific JavaScript highlighter options, I think it makes sense to add something like this.

### 1. Main reason

I validate my HTML via The Nu Html Checker, HTML Tidy, HTMLLint and HTMLHint. I try to always follow the all rules of these linters.

I add exceptions for linters, only if I have considered all variants and I have no other choice.

### 2. Variants

Is it possible to realize any of these methods?

1. Using any valid attribute in HTML, not `style`;
1. Move CSS to separate file;
1. Using JavaScript for bars generating.

### 3. Counter-arguments

#### 3.1. Strip-html

> If you feel that you are unwilling to add an exception to your linter, you can use StripHtml to strip all style attributes out of your documents. If your linter won't allow style, and you want to enforce this rule, then I see no problem with simply stripping style globally from auto-generated HTML from Markdown.

*Before*:

![Before](https://i.imgur.com/Nz1nF0N.png)

*After*:

![After](https://i.imgur.com/clAV8BV.png)

I need colored bars.

#### 3.2. Separate CSS

> While you could generate a CSS file with 100 increments to cover all percentages, I feel this is a bad use of time.

Many unused CSS is not a problem, because user can clean this. I use [**PurifyCSS**](https://github.com/purifycss/purifycss) (alternative — [**UnCSS**](https://uncss-online.com/)), to do this.

#### 3.3. JavaScript

> You could use JavaScript to set the widths, but now you are dependent on JavaScript for something so basic. If people have JavaScript disabled, or it doesn't load etc., the progress bar will still render properly by using style.

1. I think that JavaScript disabling [**is obsolete action for 2018**](https://www.reddit.com/r/webdev/comments/48z7jz/do_you_take_into_account_those_who_disable/d0nxftd/) and [**will not support users, that disable JavaScript**](https://stackoverflow.com/a/17073999/5951529);

	![Enable JavaScript](https://i.imgur.com/0rzoZIJ.png)

@cuno the inception example currently has a [single "implied" PS node](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/examples/imagenet/inception/imagenet_distributed_train.py#L88).  In hindsight, this should've been parameterized to make it more obvious.  Note that the [original code](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/examples/imagenet/inception/inception_distributed_train.py#L42-L45) required you to pass in the list of PS nodes.

So, your cluster_spec currently looks like this:
```
2017-06-19 23:23:52,543 INFO (MainThread-17003) 0: Cluster spec: {'ps': ['brain003:42158'], 'worker': ['brain002:38686']}
```

So, you have one worker and and one PS, and since the PS node is just serving parameters/tensors, it will not be spinning the GPU or doing much logging.  So, you will need to [add another worker](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/examples/imagenet/inception/inception_distributed_train.py#L109-L112) if you want to view distributed behavior.

And, assuming you get that running, you will quickly be entering the realm of optimizing/tuning the TensorFlow code itself.  Note that I did not try to tune/optimize any of the parameters, since my main focus was proving that we could port existing code to TFoS with minimal effort.  So, if you're truly interested in tuning it for your environment, there is [this comment in the distributed code](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/examples/imagenet/inception/inception_distributed_train.py#L75-L80) to be aware of, and also [this comment in the single-node multi-gpu code](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/examples/imagenet/inception/inception_train.py#L66-L68).

Hope that helps!

It is, I just wanted to make sure I understood the problem correctly. The simplest case would be to create a channel, or pull one from a pool, for each publish on each thread. Although there's a limit to the number of channel ids, they're relatively light on both sides of the connection. There's `haigha.channel_pool`, but that's old code for a transactional use case that I don't use any more. I see 3 possible ways to go with this.

1. Document that the user should publish on a channel per thread/greenlet, however they manage that.
2. Along with `1.`, improve the `ChannelPool` so that it supports the non-transactional use case by implementing a lock around the publish.
3. Encode that lock within `BasicClass`, which is an instance per channel and so can maintain its own lock relatively easily.

In either case where we handle a lock, I think we shouldn't assume that the user has monkey-patched the thread library, and so there will have to be some abstractions that allow `BasicClass` and/or `ChannelPool` to use a lock constructor that would be defined within a `Transport` implementation.

I believe standard practice for most SSL communication is to provide a way to turn it off but enable by default. S3 API and LDAP protocol support communication with and without SSL. Organisations who want to disable insecure connection will generally block the port that allows insecure communication (LDAP 389 port). Additionally, we have development environments where users want to test tools without having to deal with things like TLS and Kerberos.

The other point is when we are in an on-premise environment using tools like Minio or IBM Cloud Object Store, we need to be able to pass certs that aren't the default AWS certificates. This is a requirement for running Airflow securely in an on-premise cloud.

I would suggest defaulting to SSL enabled but allow the opportunity for users to disable it if required. As the AWS CLI does, an warning could be thrown telling users that noSSL is an insecure option.

There is a case where we do not do the correct thing, which is the fact that the logging handler is not thread local (thanks to @dmitry-prokopchenkov for pointing this out in the zipkin gitter channel). I've created this minimal example to demonstrate:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import sys
import threading
from py_zipkin.zipkin import zipkin_span

def main():
    threads = []
    for i in range(5):
        t = threading.Thread(target=test_threading)
        threads.append(t)
        t.start()

def handler(message):
    pass

def test_threading():
    with zipkin_span(service_name='main', span_name='kicking_off_threads', transport_handler=handler, sample_rate=100) as span:
        with zipkin_span(service_name='main', span_name='foobar') as child_span:
            sys.stderr.write("[{}]: root handler: {} -- child handler: {} \n".format(threading.current_thread().ident, id(span.log_handler), id(child_span.log_handler)))
    return

main()
```

There are a couple of ways to determine which files were recently created.

If you are issuing a query directly you can issue a `since` generator based query and inspect the `new` field in the results:

```js
["query", "/some/path", {
    "since": "n:lastcheck",
    "expression": ["true"],
}]
```

You'll get results like:

```js
{
    "clock": "c:80616:59",
    "is_fresh_instance": false,
    "files": [
        {
            "exists": true,
            "mode": 33188,
            "new": false,
            "name": "argv.c",
            "size": 1340,
        }
    ]
}
```

(the same results apply to the file list generated by triggers too -- see more below)

The `new` field will be true if watchman considers the file to have been created more recently than the logical point in time specified by the `since` parameter; read more about [clockspecs](http://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/clockspec.html).

Note that watchman will also consider a file as being new when it first observes the file existing; this will happen when the watch is initiated and also if it needs to [recrawl](http://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/troubleshooting.html#recrawl).  In these cases the result set is considered to be a *fresh instance* result set and you can express to watchman how you wish to be notified about it; there's a section on that that isn't easily linked to in the [since generator docs](http://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/file-query.html#since-generator)

The other way to find newly created files is to use the [since expression term](http://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/expr/since.html) and specify that you're interested in changes to either the `ctime` or `cclock` (created clock) information that watchman maintains.  This is a bit more clunky than just using the since generator instead.

We should add an expression term that selects only `new` nodes.

For now, your triggered command will need to inspect the `new` field in the returned files and react accordingly; use the [extended syntax](http://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/cmd/trigger.html#extended-syntax) and request a json input stream:

```
["trigger", "/some/path", {
   "name": "mytriggername",
   "expression": ["true"],
   "command": ["my_command"],
   "append_files": false,
   "stdin": ["name", "new"]
}]
```

Then in your implementation of `my_command`, decode stdin as JSON and parse out the information about the files.

We're not really very huge fans of automatic watches at subscription time because it changes the latency of the subscribe command very significantly on very large trees.  The recommendation is that your client explicitly use `watch-project` (rather than the deprecated `watch` command) to resolve the directory and aggregate with a parent directory.  Watch establishment is understood to have a potentially non-trivial time cost so you can use a longer timeout for this.  `watch-project` will return you a path that you can then use as the `relative_root` in your subsequent `subscribe` subscription definition. 

Overlapping watches as you describe in your most recent comment are very expensive and are aggressively avoided.  Two overlapping watches means that there will be two instances competing for IO for each change.  For large trees this will really torture your storage device.  The practical impact is more than 2x the cost of a single watch.

The nodejs client docs demonstrate the preferred flows for setting up subscriptions.  While these docs show the javascript api, the watchman server side primitives they use are the same and the same concepts apply to any watchman client:
https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/nodejs.html

Pretty much narrowed down the problem now. There are multiple things;

* -fstack-protector causes slowness, we can get rid of this in nocopts.
* -fno-omit-frame-pointer causes a small amount of slowness, we can get rid of this in nocopts too.
* c_speech_features uses -O3 by default, which can make a significant difference. -c opt only uses -O2. We can specify this in copts (I don't know that we'd want this across the entire library, but maybe - in that case, we can just recommend specifying this via --copts on the command-line).
* Specifying -march=native significantly slows mfcc down. -mtune=native is ok and provides a small speed boost. Something that gets enabled in -march=native causes a significant speed deficit in c_speech_features.

With all these addressed, libdeepspeech manages to at least match c_speech_features in performance of the same functions (and code). Just need to do some more tests and tidying before I can open a PR and close this :)

Hi, thank you for this library. Still checking out EDTF, but it looks like it solves a lot of issues I've been having. I know other implementations exist, but our main backend language is Python (and this seems like one of the more complete implementations anyway).

I have done research on querying fuzzy time intervals that might be of interest, have a look at http://samm.univ-paris1.fr/IMG/pdf/paris2014.pdf or https://www.researchgate.net/publication/266750215_Modelling_Imperfect_Time_in_Datasets. If you can read Dutch, have a look at http://lib.ugent.be/fulltxt/RUG01/001/418/820/RUG01-001418820_2010_0001_AC.pdf. We have implemented this for postgresql in a fairly efficient way, see https://github.com/OnroerendErfgoed/pgSFTI for a native C implementation and https://github.com/koenedaele/pgFTI for a pure SQL implementation (based on Postgis).

When implementing solutions like these I have found that the main hindrance is user adoption. Something like fuzzy sets (what my work is based on) is very hard to understand to most art historians and archaeologists. But I'm thinking that capturing information as EDTF and then generating Fuzzy Time Intervals from them might work very well.

I have no good solution for translating from ca. 1905, to a fuzzy time interval. (Does that mean probably in 1905, but possibly in 1904 or 1906; or does that mean somewhere between 1900 and 1910 but probably more towards the middle). I think it's inherently contextual and hard to solve for eavery use case.

Thanks for the bug report. Support for decimal types was added in 1.0.0 and this required a change in the `VARIANT` structure. I have a feeling this is what is causing the problem.

Prior to the change, `VARIANT` was a structure containing a union. Now it is a structure containing a union, which itself contains a structure with a union. This change brings the comtypes `VARIANT` definition closer to the [C++ definition](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms221627%28v=vs.85%29.aspx).

I suspect this is probably an issue with pypy's ctypes implementation. A quick google search indicates that the pypy implementation initializes fields differently than the CPython one (see [here](https://gist.github.com/marianoguerra/3281751)).

It's worth raising a bug report on pypy about this. I'll see if I can do something on this end, but any help you or the community can give with this would help speed this along.

Hello pipenv team. I do share a lot of what is said in this text, it helps a lot any developer better understand the limitations of Pipfile/pipenv when developing a library. I do want to see this text or part of this text integrated inside the official pipenv documentation.

I do have a the following amendement I would like to discuss:

For our internal python package, fully reusable, published on our internal pypi, etc, and even for my own python packages (ex: [cfgtree](https://github.com/gsemet/cfgtree), [txrwlock](https://github.com/gsemet/txrwlock), [pipenv-to-requirements](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pipenv-to-requirements)), I use a package that some may already know or even use, that abstracts these details and make life of python developer easier: PBR.
PBR basicaly reads `requirements.txt` found at the root folder of a distribution package and inject it into the `install_requires` of the `setup.py`. Developer simply needs to maintain a `requirements.txt` with loose dependency declarations. Until the support of `Pipfile` is integrated officially inside PBR, I have to use [pipenv-to-requirements](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pipenv-to-requirements) that automatically generates `requirements.txt` from `Pipfile` so that they are both synchronized and both commited in source, and PBR does the injection correctly after the distribution package has been built. I think one could use `pipenv` to generate this `requirements.txt`

I work on a [support of Pipfile](https://review.openstack.org/#/c/524436/) for PBR, so that it will be able to read the `Pipfile` (and **not** the lock file) and inject it into `install_requires` like it does with `requirements.txt`.

I do not know if other similar packages exist, because it also does other things people might not want (version from git history, auto generation of AUTHORS and ChangLog). 

But at the end, I really feel it is so easier to write, maintain and handle versioning of a Python library, I would be sad not to share this experience. I am promoting it as the "recommended" way of writing modern python libraries on my company.

I do recon that it is like "cheating" on all difficulties about library and pipenv, but at the end the work is done and developers are happy to use it so far. Part of the python training, I am giving to new python developer in my company, involves, first writing a python library maintaining `install_requires` manually, and then switching to `PBR` to see how it becomes easier (and frankly I am a fan of the [semantic commit feature](https://docs.openstack.org/pbr/latest/user/features.html#version) of pbr to automatically create the right semver version tag).

Part of the reason to declare the libraries dependencies using a dedicated file also for libraries is to be able to use tools such as readthedocs or pyup (even if pyup makes more sence when linked to an application).

I do not necessarily want to promote this method as the "standard" way of doing python package, it is actually the "OpenStack" way, but I would to share my experience, and if others have similar or contradictory experience, I'll be happy to ear them and update my point of view.

Team, what do you think of a kind of "community" section on the documentation? So that users like me can share his experience on how he uses pipenv, without necessarily the full endorsement of pipenv team?

Well, I think we still disagree somewhat. With regard to your proposal:

1. I'm OK if you want to propose a documentation change. However, it needs to be worded in a way that matches the common understanding of how virtualenv works. Your view seems pretty unusual, at least in the sense that I've never heard anyone explain virtualenv the way you do. So I wouldn't want to confuse other users by introducing terms (like "auto-activation") that only you have ever used.
2. Sorry, but I'm against any sort of "auto-activation" flag. Reasons:
  a. There's not really any such thing as "auto-activation", so it doesn't much make sense as a concept.
  b. The `sitecustomize.py` file is *solely* for the user to manage, so virtualenv shouldn't be involved with it at all.
  c. Silently modifying the environment what starting Python seems like a pretty bad idea to me, so even if it works around the issues with ansible in Homebrew, I'd consider it a *bad* workaround. I don't have a better suggestion, as I know nothing much about either system, but I'd strongly suggest finding something better.
3. I don't think we should be documenting `sitecustomize.py` hacks like you're suggesting. Anyone with enough understanding to know the risks and issues with the approach you suggest is presumably going to know enough to be able to work out the approach themselves. And if they *don't* know the risks involved, I don't wan the virtualenv docs pointing them at something that could potentially get them into trouble.

I'm sorry if this isn't the response you were hoping for, but I'm afraid I really do feel that the issue here is with how Ansible/Homebrew are interacting, and not with virtualenv. Note by the way that the "problems" you are seeing would also appear if the core Python `venv` module was used in place of virtualenv - which to me clearly points to it not being a problem with virtual environments, but rather with users' (in this case Ansible's and Homebrew's) understanding of the implications of them.

I had a very similar issues on my Windows 7 machine. Both paths, to python and to my VE had spaces in it. Couple of months before it worked fine. I found the following note on virtualenv website: Windows Notes [...] To create a virtualenv under a path with spaces in it on Windows, you’ll need the win32api library installed. The following steps lead me to success:
  1. Make sure I used pip to install virtualenv and it's the latest version (pip-7.1.0). Result: failure
  2. Install win32api. Result: failure (although there was some error at the very end of installation process).
  3. Try to install my VE in a path without spaces. Result: failure.
  4. Reinstall my Anaconda python distribution to the path that didn't contain the "[" and "]" brackets. VE had spaces in the path. Result: failure.
  5. Reinstall my Anaconda python distribution to the path that also didn't contain any spaces. VE had spaces in the path. Result: success!
So at least the Anaconda (python) installation simple, non space-pollutted path was crucial. Maybe win32api installation was also important. Not sure.

Hi,
the YAML spec doesn't say anything about this case.
Javascript and other languages only support strings as keys, YAML does.
An author of a YAML processor in such a language has to decide what to do with complex keys.

I think it's a good thing if it supports loading the YAML instead of returning an error, but there is no standard on how to convert the complex key.

In any case, information will be lost. If you convert it to a string `["Detroit, Tigers", "Chicago cubs"]`, then you have no information if this was a sequence or this exact string in the original YAML file.

If you actually want to process such YAML in a language that doesn't support complex keys, you would need a processor that supports an option on how to stringify such keys, or that devides its loading process into parsing (event based, for example, like libyaml) and constructing and provides an API, so that you can construct the data yourself.

So the behaviour of js-yaml is not a bug. Providing an option for how to stringify would probably be a nice enhancement.

I have documented the `num_workers=None` behavior.  If images are relatively small e.g. 512x512 and the chosen wavelet has short FIR filters, there is not a huge benefit to multiple workers.  I have left the default at 1 as this also avoids any potential warning on Python 2.7 for missing `futures` package. 

Here are 2 benchmarking results performed on a dual socket workstation with 16 total cores.  In both cases max_shifts are set so that there will be 16 total shifts to average.  The first is with a 512x512 grayscale image and `db1` wavelet (corresponding to filter length of 2).  In this case 4 workers performs best for ~ a factor 2 speedup:

@stefanv First off, I realize that this wasn't really the right forum for me to bring this up, so my apologies for derailing relevant discussion about this PR!

Nevertheless, I'll continue :)

My overall point is that I think the `rc` vs. `xy` argument is actually a canard: if you think about it, there are surprisingly few cases where well-written code should need to make *any* assumptions about the particular indexing convention a user is employing.

> There are two typical situations: Python code that operate on an image, that expect the image to be indexable in a certain way ([p, r, c]) but makes few or no assumptions on memory layout, and Cython code, that asks for contiguous data with a specified memory layout.

Well put. I'd just note that these two situations often get conflated for some reason, with one being used to justify the other. But in numpy, these issues are much more separable than in C or Fortran or Matlab, where indexing is more closely tied to memory layout.

Regarding the first situation: I propose that it's a "code smell" for code that doesn't care about memory layout to have any expectations about how an array should be indexed. The main exceptions are (a) dealing with color/multichannel images, where the color dimension is semantically distinct in a way that a z or t axis is not; and (b) visualization tools.

But are there other justifiable cases where code "needs" to know what my axis labels are? I agree with @jni's point that functions should just directly index into numpy arrays. However, I disagree that this requires that all functions use a `rc` rather than `xy` convention: it merely requires that functions refrain wherever possible from asserting **any** convention. For example, `scipy.ndimage` functions work equally well whether I use `xy` indexing on a fortran-contiguous array or `rc` indexing on a C-contiguous one. 

One might think that code that needs to receive / provide geometric coordinates (such as image drawing) must commit to using `xy` or `rc` coordinates, but even this isn't true. As long as the first geometric coordinate corresponds to the position along the first array axis (and so forth), then everything works out. Either `ndimage.map_coordinates` or `skimage.measure.find_contours` are decent example.

Regarding the second situation (where memory layout matters): I posit that it's rarely much more work for a function to iterate efficiently over an arbitrary strided array than to just iterate over a C-contiguous one. A few simple helper functions can make this pretty trivial, by e.g. making a view on the same memory in which the last index is guaranteed to be the fastest-moving in memory. 

In particular, I think it's silly to insist on contiguity. Say I have a contiguous array, but then want to apply an algorithm to a slice of it, say `arr[10:999, 30:750]`. That sub-array isn't contiguous, but it can still be iterated over extremely efficiently with appropriate striding. (Indeed, this would be rather more efficiently than making a contiguous copy first...) Similarly, with an RGB image, algorithms that understand striding can perform efficient, zero-copy operations on individual color planes.

Given that cython is pretty smart about strided indexing, all that's required is a helper function to provide a view on any given array in which the axes are in slow-to-fast order. Then one merely needs to write cython where the iteration is nested in the same order (i.e. the current best practice).

TL;DR previous versions of skimage `black_tophat` gives different output than the current `ndi.morphology.black_tophat` due how it handles edges, and I don't know which you would prefer be implemented in the new version of scikit-image's `black_tophat`.

In the past, scikit-image `morphology.grey.black_tophat` used `morphology.grey.closing`, which automatically pads image edges when using structuring elements with even length axes. This increases the size of edge regions, which changes how they're processed by filters which use `morphology.grey.closing` (namely, `black_tophat`). This got caught in Travis CI testing, where my new version of `grey.black_tophat` (which utilized `ndi.morphology.black_tophat`) failed a couple of the tests (see output attached) because the test's expected output requires the image to be padded, which `ndi.morphology.black_tophat` doesn't do. I'm attaching a pyplot showing the original test image, the `ndi.morphology.black_tophat` result of processing it,  the padded version, and the result that previous versions of scikit-image `black_tophat` would have provided.

_my personal opinion (feel free to ignore)_: The new arguments to `ndi.morphology.black_tophat` that I'm implementing provide several options for how to handle edges: 'reflect', 'constant', 'nearest', 'mirror', and 'wrap'. I could add one more option to that list, 'pad', which would implement padding as was implemented previously in scikit-image. If you like, I could make that the default to maintain consistent output with previous versions of scikit-image. Alternatively, the expected test output could be updated to reflect `ndi.morphology.black_tophat` outputs and documentation could be updated to indicate that the new version of scikit-image handles image edges differently for the filter, and to use `mode='pad'` to produce the output from previous versions.

Using the ``__class__`` and ``__name__`` serves a different purpose than checking the version with ``pkg_resources``. The first means we can use old versions but avoid selecting undesirable backends, which the older ``keyring`` implementations will fall back to, i.e. the backends from the ``file`` module.

The second checks that a specific package is installed with a specific version. It will not allow us to check that the ``fail`` backend  (i.e. ``'keyring.backends.fail.Keyring``) is used.

As for checking the backend availability, we could alternatively catch the exception that is raised when we call ``get_password``:

```

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

<ipython-input-3-fd4fc97143b8> in <module>()
----> 1 keyring.get_password('afp', 'vhaenel')

/data/home/vhaenel/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/keyring/core.pyc in get_password(service_name, username)
     40     """Get password from the specified service.

---> 42     return _keyring_backend.get_password(service_name, username)
     43

/data/home/vhaenel/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/keyring/backends/fail.pyc in get_password(self, service, username, password)
     16

---> 18         raise RuntimeError("No recommended backend was available")
     19 
     20     set_password = delete_pasword = get_password

RuntimeError: No recommended backend was available
```

However this is a runtime exception and I'm not sure catching that is such a good idea since it may also signify other things and isn't specific to not finding a recommended backend.

In the spirit of over-communication, I should clarify that my primary intention is *not* to add support for per-user secret keys to the package.  

I think what I really had in mind is the following: improve encapsulation of functionality in the package so that someone who wanted to implement a custom encode/decode scheme (per-user secrets, for example) could subclass relevant classes, leverage the pre-existing class methods, and then supply custom `kwargs` to the encode/decode handlers.   This would allow them to take advantage of the views + tests + configuration settings of this package instead of starting from scratch and rolling their own solution completely!

In bullet points, I would clarify my goals to be the following: 

- Encode/decode handlers that accept arbitrary `**kwargs`
- Better separation of concerns in `JSONWebTokenAuthentication`, `RefreshJSONWebTokenSerializer`, etc. such that someone could easily subclass them, leverage their pre-existing methods, and supply custom `kwargs` to the encode/decode handlers

I totally understand if those goals fall outside of the scope of this package, but wanted to share nonetheless

Maybe it's an odd style, but this is, for example, [what Django does in its form processing modules](https://github.com/django/django/blob/267dc4ad/django/forms/fields.py#L133).

With said framework, I've tried to write a simple location form field that parses user input and returns `Point` objects, and felt uncomfortable because in my subclass I had to override a lot of methods just to handle those `TypeError`s.

The problem was, in Django's `django.forms.fields.Field` (the most basic field class) a lot of methods run checks that boils down to [`value in (None, '', [], (), {})`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/267dc4ad/django/core/validators.py#L15). I doubt if many developers would expect that `x in [None, ""]` would raise an exception, the expected behavior is probably `False` with "no, the value isn't in the list" meaning.

That's why the suggestion.

@arnavk I'm suggesting firing a signal so anyone can deal with it in whatever way they want, since this filters thing is fairly specific and we get to a point where configuration is too complex to solve ever need.

There's a couple of approaches we could do, though some have complexity:

1. We could fire a signal when Raven is configured
2. We could fire a signal when setup_logging is called
3. Flask-only: We could fire a signal in one or both of the above

The problem with 1/2 is that we'd need our own signal framework in place. It's not a complex thing, but it's probably overkill at this point. Whereas with #3, we can just require blinker like we already do, and wire, at the very least, setup_logging to fire something like a logging_configured signal. Your app could then just bind to that and add any filters/whatever from there.

Here are some stats.  I let one file run to completion.  So 5000 total objects tested. (10,000 drawn, but half are just 90 degree rotations of the other half, so only 5000 were tested for rejection.)
- 35771 = number of times a stamp was redrawn (possibly for multiple reasons)
- 30710 = number of rejections for the resolution cut by Great3Reject
- 9954 = number of rejections for the SNR cut by Great3Reject
  - 3029 = SNR < 17
  - 6925 = SNR > 100
- 3763 = number of rejections for the flux in stamp < 0.99 * total flux

So resolution is the biggest category of rejections.  But SNR > 100 is probably an important one too, since I bet these are larger objects that take more time for their FFTs.

And a total of 40771 stamps were drawn with a success rate of 5000/40771 = 12% which sounds compatible with your numbers above, since this file (the first one) happened to have a particularly large PSF fwhm = 0.97 arcsec.

There are two options, and which one to go is depends on what you want to achieve.

1. It seems that now you are running pgbouncer in front of each postgres node, but you can run it in front of haproxy.

2. another option would be to update confd [template](https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/extras/confd/templates/haproxy.tmpl#L24). `{{base (replace (index (split $data.conn_url "/") 2) "@" "/" -1)}}` this construction extracts 'host:port' from `conn_url`, which is stored in the `/service/cluster-name/members/member-name`. You have to change it in such way, that it will extract only host. Port in your case will be always 6543

1. I will create fix for dynamic ttl/loop_wait
2. I thought about that myself. I am not sure if we want to. I view the
watchdog as a feature for people who are concerned about corretness first
and availability second. When we are not master the watchdog can act as a
safeguard layer against misconfiguration - e.g. some other script runs on
the system and makes postgresql master behind Patroni's back, Patroni will
have the server rebooted if the situation can't be fixed quickly enough. On
the other hand, rebooting standbys somebody does a VM migration type of
operation is not exactly user friendly.
3. Main reason for the current way is that I wanted to send the keepalive
ASAP and so I designed it from the bottom up and went through all code
paths to find the earliest safe point to send the keepalive at. With
default settings the timing slack is uncomfortably small considering we are
occasionally waiting in the main HA loop. I agree that the current setup is
quite messy. I'm analyzing the whole setup again to figure out if it could
be simplified somehow. I will post an update with the results.

Short answer: No.

Long answer: We do not provide a way to validate status code of responses. We do that because developers using Connexion would need to define all response codes that their expect the application to possibly return ever, unless Connexion would throw an error. 

That behavior could be confusing. If not defined a response code of error (like 500) Connexion would return an different error saying that 500 is not defined in the API spec. That said, the developers would need to probably add to all endpoints of the API spec a few basic response codes that are not really important to the understand of their APIs. We would probably see 404, 500, 503 in all endpoints plus the success cases like 200, 201 etc.

If you see a value on that kind of validation you can send a PR to Connexion to add this kind of strict validation. We would consider accepting if it is implemented as an optional configuration.

The `client.thing(id).get()` proposal seems to have come out of the blue. It has many problems:
 - As a user, I would prefer to write `client.get_thing(id)`.
 - `Thing.get` is naturally a class method. Seeing it be an instance method would surprise me. If the class method were ever desired in the future, the instance method would get in the way.
 - How would `list()` fit in? Badly, I think.
 - `thing.reload()` returns `None` to emphasize that it is side-effectful, like `list.reverse()`. This consideration typically outweighs enabling method chaining, except where method chaining is a key element of the interface (e.g., in building database queries).
 - `client.thing(id).get()` forces the client library to tolerate partly constructed objects. If certain attributes need to be defined before `save()` can be called, making them required parameters of the factory (and the constructor) would make this apparent in the method signature. Informing the user of the usage error through an exception raised by `save()` is not optimal.

I would be wary of trying too hard to define a one-size-fits-all CRUD pattern. Different APIs are different:
 - Some resources are about named objects offering little more than object identity (e.g., PubSub topics), sometimes with names assigned by the client, and sometimes with names assigned by the service. Some resources have associated second-class properties like ACLs or "metadata" (e.g., Cloud Storage blobs). Other resources have lots of essential attributes (e.g., metric descriptors). Some are nothing more than saved collections of attributes (e.g., Stackdriver groups, which are really just saved filters).
 - Some resources are mutable. Some can only be created and deleted (e.g., metric descriptors). Others are read-only (e.g., managed resource descriptors).

wrt performance; my geometric mg code is formulated in terms of, and dominated by scipy.sparse.csr_matrix multiplications; which are implemented in cython. I am considering a simple isotropic poisson problem here; in 2d, either as a regular grid or on the surface of a subdivided icosahedron. Hi, to participate in the discussion, I understand why you consider it bad since it can disclose security details, but, for another point of view, if you are coding microservices, it is really helpful to get almost the real exception when doing calls, so you can write code that acts differently to different exceptions. 
The thing is, here there is two opposite points of view, a) you silence every exceptions and return a generic error code but for those that you choose, b) you pass exceptions as-is except for those that you choose to silence (try...except: raise GenericException("Something wrong") in the calee)
I'm more an adept of the second option (Explicit is better than implicit, never silence exception unless explicitly silenced). That said, maybe we could change the doc to explicitly tell about the exceptions and security implications of it.

Maybe there could also be a way to setup the router to intercept every exceptions (or based from source,destination or both) an do things with it (i.e. convert it to generic return code and/or log it) but this is changing the router to add some business logic in it.

@nemesisdesign Yes, django_hstore uses the code which is already deprecated (but not yet completely removed) in 1.7 resulting in breakages.

This is whole source code of [transaction.is_managed()](https://github.com/django/django/blob/stable/1.7.x/django/db/transaction.py#L112-114):
```python
def is_managed(using=None):
    warnings.warn("'is_managed' is deprecated.",
        RemovedInDjango18Warning, stacklevel=2)
```
Then there is [this](https://github.com/djangonauts/django-hstore/blob/master/django_hstore/query.py#L57-59) :
```python
    if not transaction.is_managed(using=self.db):  # always returns true
        transaction.enter_transaction_management(using=self.db)  # this line breaks any kind of transaction.atomic() call there could be
        forced_managed = True
```

I'm able to trigger this error whenever there is a file generated by conda 4.3.28 in
`<path-to-activated-environment>/conda-meta` and then if conda is somehow downgraded to 4.3.27.

@Lemmaho Perhaps these commands should help?
```
rmdir /s /q <path-to-activated-environment>\conda-meta
conda clean -i
rmdir /s /q <path-to-miniconda|anaconda-installation>\pkgs\cache
conda install conda=4.3.27 -y
```

So, for @jennirinker 's case, it would be:
```
rmdir /s /q C:\Users\jrinker\Miniconda3\conda-meta
conda clean -i
rmdir /s /q C:\Users\jrinker\Miniconda3\pkgs\cache
conda install conda=4.3.27 -y
```

If the environment is not the root environment, then the conda-meta directory will be someplace in:
``<path-to-miniconda|anaconda-installation>\envs\<environment-name>\conda-meta``

NOTE: After removing files from conda-meta, conda would probably not be aware of the packages installed in that environment. The packages will be there, just conda wouldn't know about them. A quick re-installation of the installed package should probably suffice. If you wish to be overly careful about this, then  just delete those json files in conda-meta which have timestamp in decimals. 

For Linux/macOS users, delete the files which have the timestamp in decimals using:
```
grep -l '"timestamp": [0-9]\+\.'  ~/<path-to-activated-environment>/conda-meta/*.json | xargs rm -f
```
Then, do:
```
conda clean -i
rm -fr <path-to-miniconda|anaconda-installation>/pkgs/cache
conda install conda=4.3.27
```
If you are advanced conda users who have configured pkg cache directory or environment directory using the parameters `envs_dirs` or `pkgs_dirs`, you will have to delete the cache or conda-meta/*<bad-files>.json from the corresponding directories.

The issue is when lpath is Unix based path (starting with /) and current OS is Windows (paths starting with C:\)

So in your scenarios, point 3,4,5,6 are behaving as per expectation and is the right behavior.
1,2 should have behaved in similar manner.
When you start the lpath with "/" the download will start from the root directory followed by lpath. In windows root with "/" is the current directory's drive letter. So in point 1, directories should be saved in C:/testfolder/test/
And when you start the lpath with "./" then download will start from current directory followed by lpath. So in point 2 the directories should be saved in C:/scriptlocation/testfolder/test/

`URL.query_string` did end up returning what I expected so there were no issues there.

However, when it came to `URL.query` to turn it into a `MultiDict` that's when things went awry. It turns out that the stdlib function, `parse_qsl` does not handle this case very well:

```py
>>> from urllib.parse import parse_qsl
>>> parse_qsl('test=foo;bar')
[('test', 'foo')]
```

And this is what `yarl` uses to help construct.

You raise a good point. I am coming around to see the need for the version bump being outside of the PR. Here's an example of PRs submitted to the same module, all with independent purposes and no conflicts between them (from what I remember):

* https://github.com/OCA/connector/pull/216
* https://github.com/OCA/connector/pull/215
* https://github.com/OCA/connector/pull/213

None of these PRs raised the patch version in the manifest. Had we tried to do so, the respective authors would have had to watch for other authors' PRs to possibly get merged in front of their's.

Hi Riandy,

*A* is intended as a topic administrator's permission. *A* is for _Administrator_. It's a permission which allows the *A* user to manage permissions of other subscribers of the given topic (P2P or Group). It seems appropriate for the case of P2P topics where each user manages permissions of the other user. 

The two participants of a P2P topic cannot invite other users to the topic because of the way the name of a P2P topic is composed. It's based on a concatenation of the IDs of the two participants. So the *A* permission will only affect the two members of the P2P topic.

re: why not use `ga`? Good question, I should explain more.

The purpose of this feature would be to standardize how I indicate my product's test environment.

I am pretty sure this is at least partially a duplicate of #88. I think the dateutil timezones work because the utc offsets are more lazily calculated than in pytz, where they are cached after the first invocation. I'll have to dig in a bit more deeply to make sure, but this may be an issue with pytz - or at least just an improper usage of pytz time zones.

I'm guessing that the correct behavior here is that the rule should generate 12 dates with the 8th of each month, at 19:00. One easy solution is to start `local_time` as a naive `datetime` with no time zone and apply the timezone afterwards, e.g.:

```python
import pytz
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil import rrule as rr
from dateutil.tz import gettz

try:
    from itertools import imap
except ImportError:
    imap = map

BERLIN = pytz.timezone("Europe/Berlin")

local_time = datetime(2015, 4, 8, 19, 0)
local_time_du = datetime(2015, 4, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=gettz("Europe/Berlin"))
local_time_pt = BERLIN.localize(datetime(2015, 4, 8, 19, 0))

rrn = rr.rrule(freq=rr.MONTHLY, count=12, dtstart=local_time)       # Naive rule
rrdu = rr.rrule(freq=rr.MONTHLY, count=12, dtstart=local_time_du)   # tzfile
rrpt = rr.rrule(freq=rr.MONTHLY, count=12, dtstart=local_time_pt)   # tzfile
rrm = imap(BERLIN.localize, rrn)   # Map the localizing function to the naive

comp_rrule = lambda rr1, rr2: [cdu == cpt for cdu, cpt in zip(rr1, rr2)]

print("Compare pytz to dateutil:")
print(comp_rrule(rrpt, rrdu))

print("Compare naive->pytz mapping to dateutil:")
print(comp_rrule(rrm, rrdu))
```
This returns:
```python
Compare pytz to dateutil:
[True, True, True, True, True, True, True, False, False, False, False, False]
Compare naive->pytz mapping to dateutil:
[True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
```
I think there are other options as well, such as wrapping pytz in a `tzinfo` subclass, or subclassing `pytz.tzinfo.DstTzInfo`.

I've had a similar problem. The solution I've came up with is to use meta-classes and create schemas on the fly, like so:

```python
# Function for creating schemas
def SchemaFactory(model, fields={}):
    dict = fields.copy()
    dict['Meta'] = type('Meta', (), {'model': model})

    return type(model.__name__ + 'Schema', (ma.ModelSchema,), dict)

# Sample usage
Schema = SchemaFactory(UnitTopic, {
  'topic': fields.Nested(TopicSchema),
  'unit': fields.Nested(UnitSchema)
})

schema = Schema(many=True)
schema.dump(unit_topics).data
```

So, you get to control what gets extended, by providing a fields.Nested entry to the factory. It might be a bit hacky, but seems to work.

The problem is, that senza assumes a DNS structure like `{SUBDOMAIN}.{HOSTED_ZONE}` (e.g. `my-app.example.org`) at many places, which was completely right for the "old" CNAME records.

Now with the "alias" feature, it is possible to have A records with name={HOSTED_ZONE} (just `example.org`), but `senza create` fails to produce the correct Cloud Formation template, e.g. when setting the name of a WeightedDnsElasticLoadBalancer equal to the hosted zone name (e.g. `MainDomain: example.org`).

However we managed to create the records with pure cloud formation:

```yaml
Resources:
  AppLoadBalancerMainDomain:
    Type: AWS::Route53::RecordSet
    Properties:
      AliasTarget:
        DNSName:
          Fn::GetAtt:
            - AppLoadBalancer
            - DNSName
        HostedZoneId:
          Fn::GetAtt:
            - AppLoadBalancer
            - CanonicalHostedZoneNameID
      HostedZoneName: example.org.
      Name: example.org.
      SetIdentifier: "example.org-{{SenzaInfo.StackVersion}}"
      Type: A
      Weight: 0
  AppLoadBalancerVersionDomain:
    Type: AWS::Route53::RecordSet
    Properties:
      AliasTarget:
        DNSName:
          Fn::GetAtt:
            - AppLoadBalancer
            - DNSName
        HostedZoneId:
          Fn::GetAtt:
            - AppLoadBalancer
            - CanonicalHostedZoneNameID
      HostedZoneName: example.org.
      Name: "{{SenzaInfo.StackVersion}}.example.org."
      Type: A
```

This works fine for stack creation, but later when it comes to switching the traffic using senza, it fails again 😭

A few follow-ups,

2. How different do you imagine the signature for structure featurizers being than that for composition? My original picture was to have all of the featurizers, regardless of what sort of data they take as input, follow the usage pattern:

``` 
f = Featurizer()
# For a single material
features = f.featurize(<object describing material>)
# For many materials
features = f.featurize_all(<list of objects describing materials>)
```

The type of object used to describe a material would, of course, vary between different featurizers. Right now, given how you've refactored AbstractFeaturizer and the current implementations in structures.py, it seems like they would be used like:

```
f = Featurizer(<object describing material>)
# For a single material
features = f.featurize()
# For many materials
features = [Featurizer(m).featurize() for m in <list describing materials>]
```

Does either one of these capture your plan for the structure features? I realize you haven't implemented the `featurize` function yet, but want to make sure Jiming, you, and I agree to a similar pattern before any of us gets too far.

3. I think you make a good point about the difficulties in tracking down the source of property data if we create some scheme for merging them. Also considering your point about keeping AbstractData easy to implement for new sources, I agree with should keep it as-is.

For versions prior to 1.6.0 we used the `tensorflow-tensorboard` package name.  Since pip doesn't have a notion of package renames, it's not aware that `tensorboard 1.6.0` should replace `tensorflow-tensorboard 1.5.0`.  See here under "Known issues" for details:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/releases/tag/1.6.0

Once TF 1.6 is out, installing it should only install `tensorboard` and not `tensorflow-tensorboard`.  In the mean time, as long as it's working, it's harmless to leave `tensorflow-tensorboard` installed.

> Care to explain how? The only thing that matters is that parameters are in sync once you call acquire.
If the card is out of date with the driver at any other time it can never be reflected in data.
It practice the old system was broken. the code was filled with _set_update and to work around the inability to call get.

The thing I wanted to protect the user from is getting the value of a parameter which is not synced to the board. This is not only a risk when calling acquire (although an important one) but also when logging results. Or sometimes I read values from the alazar to determine how my acquisition should run. If i forgot to call config, my acquisition is screwed up. Keeping track per parameter and forbidding a get of the parameter until it is synced saved me from some errors in the past

Just to clarify, the 0.7s/14 kb of data you talk about is your ‘integration time’ in an experiment. How long an integration time you select depends on the signal to noise of the experiment. The 0.7s/14 kb here you refer to is probably from a single acquisition rather than the streaming mode and so it corresponds to your Alazar’s onboard memory. Each card has a different onboard memory. In streaming mode, you are no longer limited by onboard memory as it operates as a FIFO stack and pulls the data off onto the hard drive as fast as you can take it. I guess when I say infinite, I mean ‘a very large number' that is set by the user’s system parameters (hard drive size etc). 

For example, a ‘loop' experiment I could run is: 

At each step in gate voltage, use the Alazar to integrate for 1 hour and store the raw data. Therefore the ‘bytes’ is something on order GB for each point in the loop.

First, let's agree on performance metrics that should be measured: 

1) Read/write speed 
2) Memory usage 
3) CPU load 

We can distinguish writing actions and retrieval actions. I think in the first instance we should focus on how well we can write data to the database.   

One requirement I got while talking to experimentalists is that the data write speed should be at least 100 MB/s. This value is based on the maximum rate at which data is generated and on the desire to not keep piling up data in memory. 

Also, we need to agree on the hardware to perform the profiling on: This, of course, needs to happen on the computers where the measurements are performed (and not on the local development computer)

What happens when you run the application longer? Does it run out of memory and crash? Or does the memory usage plateau?

The object store does not evict objects until it fills up. So it will use up more and more memory until it gets full at which point it will start evicting old objects. This may be what you're seeing.

One thing that's currently not exposed through the API, but could be tried, is to start the object store with less memory. As a quick hack, you could try modifying this line https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/services.py#L629 to pass in a different amount of memory.

Let me know if that has the desired effect.

There currently isn't a way to evict objects earlier, though it would be nice to provide a way to run with a more aggressive eviction policy or something like that. We've actually experimented with implementing referencing counting in the past to figure out when an object will no longer be used, but it ended up adding more overhead than we wanted. But some things like this could probably be done in the future.

I have found a workaround which may have some serious side effects. All I can say is that it seems to resolve the issue.


```
def create_test_remote(foo:
    # Change from worker mode to script mode for a moment in order to trick
    # worker into exporting function definiton
    original_mode = ray.worker.global_worker.mode
    ray.worker.global_worker.set_mode(ray.worker.SCRIPT_MODE)

    @ray.remote
    def _test_remote(foo):        
        return True
    ray.worker.global_worker.set_mode(original_mode)

    out_id = _test_remote.remote(foo)
    return out_id
```
This works because this causes the worker to publish the remote function definition rather than trying to look it up in the cache.

This is soooo cool !

This allow simple coroutine creation from within `async with` statements: 

```python

def with_server(fn):

    async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
            server_address = await nursery.start(run_server)
            nursery.start_soon(lambda: fn(server_address, *args, **kwargs))

    return wrapper


async def test_connect_to_server():
    @with_server
    async def tester(address):
        conn = await open_connection(address)
        res = await conn.ping()
        assert res == 'ok'

    await tester()
```

becomes:

```python
@trio._util.acontextmanager
async def start_server():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        server_address = await nursery.start(start_server)
        yield server_address


async def test_connect_to_server():
    async with start_server() as server_address:
        conn = await open_connection(address)
        res = await conn.ping()
        assert res == 'ok'
```

Obviously only drawback is the second solution makes use of `trio._util.acontextmanager` which is not exposed by trio. This is more a trouble of Python itself though given it doesn't provide (yet ?) a simple way to do async context manager like `contextlib.contextmanager`.
Considering this usecase, maybe it would be useful to move this `acontextmanager` function to it own repo (or make it part of `async_generator` package) ?

I'd take a second opinion on this. Several projects like ipyparallel, boto, and even the C Programming Language book start with a beginners section followed by the reference section. My worry is that we'll make the userguide needlessly tedious by pushing class docs into it. For eg, classes documentation like the DFK contain info on methods that the user is never expected to use. The pythonApp class is simple because it has very few methods.

At it's core parsl does just a few things, I hope that the userguide is easy to run through and I worry that for a new user adding class docs may not help in the initial reading.

If the doc is outdated, that's a doc bug. The user facing docs still have to be written and maintained, and we realistically can rely on the class docs only as a supplement, esp for a new user. 

Perhaps we could either link, or include just specific interfaces rather than the class definition ?

runScript(String) expects a path to a filename.  [It is looking for a file.](https://github.com/ninia/jep/blob/dev_3.7/src/jep/pyembed.c#L1462)  It primarily exists to enable a command line execution of the jep script, for example `python someFile.py` has an equivalent `jep someFile.py`.

`eval` works on one line at a time unless you're in interactive mode as set on the `JepConfig`.  Please see the [javadoc for eval](https://github.com/ninia/jep/blob/dev_3.7/src/jep/Jep.java#L537).
